So that just means where am I sending the traffic to? What's the URL where we finally end up after clicking on an ad? Used to be called a destination URL. So if you've done AdWords a little bit in the past and you're sort of revisiting it and you're wondering why the name has changed, it used to be called destination URL. It's now called final URL. Then you need two headlines. They're 30 characters, the maximum of 30 characters each, headline one and headline two. What's called a display path. You'll see that on the example in just a second. There's a thing called a display URL, which you no longer have any control over. Google takes the top level domain, the bit before the forward slash, from your final URL, the example.com slash page one. It takes the example.com and makes out the display URL. Then you get the chance of putting another couple of chunks of text after that. So forward slash zebra, forward slash onion. I don't know why you'd have that, but you could if you wanted to. As long as they're not more than 15 characters in length each. So path one and path two, 15 characters each. Then you get an 80 character description, where you can obviously sell the benefits of your product or service. And that all looks a little bit like this. So at the top, when you're adding this to your Google AdWords account.

## [06:02 - 09:04]

So if I give you this example, let's focus on the left-hand side first. If we're running a search campaign, we've chosen our keywords. We've already done that in the last module. Now we get to write a couple of ads, and we can test these against each other, and there could be massive changes in there, or there could be very, very small changes. You can see the headlines. There's not a huge difference here. Testing Brian Tracy versus Brian Tracy International. The paths are the same. Path one and path two is the same. The description in this case is the main difference. So we're testing different descriptions there. And we'll talk about how we choose this in a measure of success and so on later on, but ultimately, which one makes you the most profit? There's lots of different measures of success. The main one is going to be how many people click on each ad? For every hundred people that see this ad, how many people click the ad? That's going to be one measure. The other one might be how many leads do I get for every hundred people that see the ad, or for every hundred people that click the ad? How many leads do I get? Or maybe even the cost of the lead, but generally what percentage of people clicked? That's probably the easiest one to use when you're starting out. Now, I'm not going to talk about the display network or banner ads in this course, but just to give you an idea, you can do the exact same thing over on the display network and you can test banner ads against each other just the same way as you test text ads against each other. And little changes there can make a huge, huge difference. Now, for this client, obviously, we're going to test pictures of the man himself. Usually that works rather than showing a picture of someone that's not him, but you never know. And you never know which picture is going to work or which headline is going to work. So the key, the key to the whole game, the thing you have to remember with Google AdWords is you must, must, must test. I actually say that the main reason you have a Google AdWords account is so that you can test different things. It's a side benefit of Google AdWords that you get all this lovely traffic to your website. The main reason that you have AdWords is to test and be a real marketer and to test one thing against another so that you can do more of the good stuff and less of the bad stuff. Let's talk about things that you can therefore test with your search ads. By far, the most important thing that you want to test is the headline of your ad. It's been said by people smarter than me in the past, you know, the headline of an ad is 80% of the ad. If they don't read the headline, they won't read the ad. Certainly, the headline of a landing page is the key thing on that landing page. The job of the headline is to get them to read the first line.

## [09:04 - 12:05]

The job of the first line is to get them to read the rest of the first paragraph. The job of the first paragraph is to get them to read the next and so on and so on and so on. So it goes with ads. Make sure you test different headlines. So even if you can't think of anything else to test, make a copy of your ad, copy it into the same ad group. And again, I'm going to talk about structure in the next thing and talk about ad groups, but you create a copy of your ad and you go in and you change one of the headlines. So you can be testing headline A against headline B and you probably heard the expression AB testing. And that's what we mean by AB testing. Testing thing A against thing B. It could be one ad against another. It could be one landing page against another. Back in the day, we used to be able to test domain names and test one domain name against another. That's much, much harder to do that. This day and age, we won't worry about that now. Test the call to action. What are you going to ask somebody to do in that ad? Are you asking for the... Are you telling them to come and download a PDF? Are you asking them to sign up for a free consultation? Are you asking them to make a booking or call you on the phone? What is it that you are asking them to do? And be very explicit about that call to action. Test different benefits. Now, we could probably talk about benefits for an hour and the difference between features and benefits. Now, established in 1973, that's a feature. That's not a benefit. But if you go, so what? compare, imagine, uncover, increase, customize. And that's just the start of it. You'll find blog posts with hundreds of these action words. Use action words in your ad copy.
